Synadia · audit for the agent era

Agent Audit

A flight recorder for your AI agents.

Durable, semantic audit of everything your agents say over NATS — reconstructed from the wire, without touching a byte of production traffic.

In one line Repoint your agents' NATS URL at a transparent proxy, and get a clean, durable, queryable record of every agent interaction — who called whom, what was asked, what came back — provable, and impossible to read any other way.

  2. It's not tracing

01 — The problem

Your agents stopped chatting. They started doing.

AI agents now call each other, call tools, and take real actions on real data — autonomously, at machine speed. That's the point of them. It's also the problem.

Those interactions are ephemeral, streamed in pieces, and scattered across the wire. When an agent gives a harmful answer, leaks a secret, or runs up a bill, there is usually no faithful record of what actually happened — only logs you hoped someone added to the right harness.

What was said

The exact prompt, and the full answer that came back.

Did it leak

Did a secret or PII go out on the wire?

Prove it

Which payload flowed — tamper-evident, for compliance.

Who called whom

Caller → agent, how often, and is it alive right now?

Today, the only way to answer these is to instrument every agent, SDK, and harness by hand — inconsistently, and only where someone remembered to.

02 — Not tracing

Not a span that happened — the exchange that occurred.

It's tempting to file this under tracing. Don't. A trace tells you a span happened: a request started, some chunks went by, it ended. You learn that it occurred and how long it took.

This produces something different — a semantic record: the actual interaction, reconstructed. Who called whom, what was asked, the full reassembled answer, the latencies, the status.

A trace is one thing you could derive from this data. It isn't the data.

03 — How it works

A transparent proxy, in the data path.

The obvious approach — connect to NATS as a client and subscribe to agents.> — is structurally broken for this. To see a streamed reply, a subscriber must dynamically subscribe to each temporary _INBOX, which registers interest on the server and perturbs no-responder detection, cluster routing, and queue distribution. It also races the agent's first chunk and loses it.

So Agent Audit isn't a subscriber. It sits on the wire as a transparent TCP proxy. Agents connect to it; it forwards every byte to the real NATS server and back, verbatim. The reply-inbox chunks flow through it as ordinary frames — observed in order, in full, with zero subscriptions of its own.

Installing it is repointing one URL. Your existing NATS credentials relay straight through — the proxy holds none of its own — and the server sees exactly the CONNECT, SUB, and PUB the agent sent. TLS is terminated on both legs so the frames can be parsed, then re-encrypted upstream; one set of agent creds covers the whole hop.

04 — Why it's safe in the path

In the path — but incapable of harming it.

A tool in the data path can break production. So this one is built so that it can't — three properties, enforced by design:

Observe-only

Can't alter a frame

Every handler records and returns. It cannot deny, mutate, or block a single byte of traffic.

Fail-open

Can't slow you down

The audit path is async. Under pressure it drops records — and counts them — rather than ever back-pressuring the pipe.

Never subscribes

Can't perturb NATS

It observes frames as they pass. The server's interest graph stays exactly as the agents left it.

These aren't aspirations — they're invariants the test suite proves. 148 tests, green under the race detector: every handler returns without mutating a frame, a saturated sink drops without stalling the connection, and a recording tee on the backend confirms zero injected subscriptions.

05 — The semantic engine

From a stream of frames to one record.

Between the wire and the audit log sits the semantic engine. It does not log raw frames, and it never emits individual chunks. It watches the frames go by and rebuilds meaning:

1
Correlate — match each request to its streamed reply, keyed by the reply inbox.
2
Reassemble — fold the response chunks back into one complete answer.
3
Bind identity — resolve who called whom: caller → agent / owner / name.
4
Track liveness — fold heartbeats into online / offline transitions.

Then it emits one record per exchange. Because request/reply is symmetric, whichever end is proxied yields a complete view — a record is tagged served (this connection is the agent answering) or called (the caller).

06 — Anatomy of a record

One line. The whole story.

Every record is one JSON line. The densest is the prompt.exchange — the reconstructed request → response, captured live from one real exchange:

It carries who (caller → agent identity), what (the subject, the full prompt and reassembled response), and how it went (ack and total latency, chunk counts, status). Two fields do quiet, important work.

Provenance — prove what flowed, even storing nothing

Every body carries a sha256 of the original bytes — present at every audit level, even metadata-only with no body stored at all. You can prove an agent received or emitted a given payload by hash alone: cheap, tamper-evident provenance that scales from "metadata" to "full fidelity" per your appetite.

Redaction — capture the meaning, not the secret

Secrets and PII can be scrubbed before a record is written — built-in rules for email, jwt, apikey, card, ssn, nats-seed, plus your own regex. The hash still covers the original, so you keep provenance without storing the secret. And it touches only the audit copy — the proxied bytes stay byte-exact.

in  email it to a.dev@acme.com — key sk-AB12…
out email it to [REDACTED] — key [REDACTED]  · sha256 unchanged
07 — Where it lands

A durable stream you can replay, query, and alert on.

Records go to a JSONL file and/or a durable JetStream stream — on its own connection, even a different cluster or cloud than your agents talk through. The audit store is decoupled and relocatable.

That last property matters: a slow or down audit server sits behind a wall from production. The publisher drops, counts, and reconnects on its own — auditing being degraded never degrades agent traffic.

08 — See it live

Everything your agents did — live and decoupled.

agentview is a standalone, read-only viewer. It reads the same audit stream — from the JSONL file or JetStream — and serves a live dashboard over SSE. It's a separate process, never in the data path, so it can't perturb anything.

09 — Two layers

This is the hard half: the data layer.

"Auditability" is really two layers, and it's worth being clear which this is.

Layer 2 · what you build

Audit & governance

Filters, anomaly rules, stat runs, retention policies, alerts.

Layer 1 · this

The semantic record layer

Clean, semantic, durable, provable records of what agents actually did.

The compliance and governance audit is layer two, built on top. You can't do it without layer one — and layer one is the hard part nobody else has for free. This is the data layer.

10 — Why NATS

Meaning comes from speaking the protocol.

The value comes from speaking the Synadia Agent Protocol over NATS. That's how a record can say "this agent got work, this agent replied, this agent was called to do activity." The semantics come for free, because they're already on the wire.

The right way to grow it is up the NATS stack. As A2A and richer agent protocols ride on NATS, Agent Audit adds decoders for them — the same vantage point, more meaning.

▲  A2A · richer agent protocols

━  NATS — one vantage point
Repoint a URL. Get a provable audit trail.

Working and proven end-to-end, locally and against Synadia Cloud (NGS): transparent proxy, TLS MITM with transparent auth, structured JSONL + durable JetStream, prompt-exchange reconstruction, identity & liveness, $SRV discovery, redaction, and the live dashboard. Next: Prometheus metrics, a health endpoint, graceful drain, YAML config.
